segments.s623-g121: جهانجوی is a nominative noun phrase 'the world-seeker / ambitious man.' از جهاندار یاد is the complement: یاد کردن + از means 'to remember/think of someone'; کرد is the past-tense auxiliary 'did' used here in the compound verb یاد کرد ('remembered'). Word order is subject — prepositional object (از + noun) — verb, a common poetic SOV inversion.
segments.s623-g122: که introduces a subordinate/clausal complement ('that' / 'who'). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / throughout' modifying دید 'saw' (simple past). زان‌گونه 'in that manner/so' + شاد 'happy' form a resultative phrase; the copula is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the clause reads 'he had seen the whole world and was thus happy.'
segments.s623-g65: بکوشید is a 2nd-person-plural imperative (classical/polite) meaning ‘strive’ or ‘make an effort’. The conjunction و simply links the two imperatives. پیمان ما places the object (our pact) before the verb; ما is the possessive/agent pronoun attached to پیمان. مشکنید is likewise an imperative plural formed on the root شکن- with the negative sense here ‘do not break/violate’ (in older Persian the negative imperative can appear in this simple form).
segments.s623-g66: This clause lists objects before the verb: پی و بیخ و پیوند (three nouns joined by و) are the things ordered to be removed; بد is an adjective qualifying (primarily) پیوند or the whole series as ‘bad/evil’. برکنید is an imperative plural from برکندن meaning ‘uproot/tear out/remove’. In classical word order Persian places the direct objects before the verb, so these nouns are the objects of برکنید.
segments.s623-g67: به یزدان پناهید is an imperative plural construction: به + noun (یزدان, ‘God’) followed by پناهید (imperative ‘take refuge’ / ‘seek refuge’). The conjunction و links that command to the next: فرمان کنید, where فرمان is the noun ‘command, order’ and کنید (imperative/optative of کردن) means ‘issue’ or ‘perform’ — together ‘give orders’ or ‘set a decree’. The verb forms are present/imperative in polite/plural address.
segments.s623-g68: روان را uses روان (‘soul, spirit’) plus the object marker را to mark it definite/accusative. به مهرش is a prepositional phrase ‘into/to his love’, with مهرش a bound form (مهر + ـش = ‘his love/affection’). گروگان کنید uses گروگان (‘hostage/pledge’) as predicate nominal and کنید (do/make) to mean ‘make (the soul) a hostage/pledge to his love’. The structure is verb-final idea expressed by placing object and complement before the verb, common in Persian.
segments.s623-g69: مجویید is a negative imperative/optative for the 2nd-person-plural meaning ‘do not seek’ (classical Persian negative form). It governs the direct object آزار (‘harm, trouble’). همسایگان follows as the object of آزار. Word order places object(s) before the (omitted) verb in classical usage; here the negative verb precedes the object but the meaning is straightforward: ‘do not seek harm to the neighbors.’
segments.s623-g70: هم آن بزرگان و پرمایگان is an appended phrase clarifying who else must not be harmed: هم ‘also / likewise’, آن ‘those’, بزرگان ‘the great ones’ and پرمایگان ‘the rich/men of substance or nobles’. The list is coordinated by و and continues the prohibition from the previous clause; word order is determiner (آن) + plural noun.
segments.s623-g71: هرانکس که is a standard relative/head clause meaning ‘whoever’ (هر + آنکس). ناچیز (‘insignificant’) and بد (‘bad, evil’) are predicative adjectives describing that person; they appear before the verbal predicate. چیره گشت is a periphrastic past formation: چیره (dominant/prevailing) + گشت (‘became’) = ‘became dominant/overcame’. The sequence gives: ‘whoever, being insignificant and bad, became dominant.’
segments.s623-g72: وز here is an archaic/contracted particle (in the text it functions like و + ز = ‘and from / and beyond’), followed by اندازه کهتری ‘the measure/limit of lowliness/inferiority’ and برگذشت ‘he passed over/exceeded’. برگذشت is a past tense verb meaning ‘passed beyond / transgressed’. The whole clause means ‘and he passed beyond the proper measure of lowliness (i.e. exceeded the bounds of his inferiority). Note: the provided sense list for وز does not match this particle use, so that item is marked as unknown.
segments.s623-g17: بکوشید is a 2nd-person plural polite imperative (ب + کوشید) meaning 'strive/try.' تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'). رنجها (object) precedes the verb; کم کنید is a causative construction where کم ('less/fewer') combines with کنید (2pl subjunctive/imperative of کردن) to mean 'make [them] less' or 'diminish [them].' Word order is typical poetic Persian: purpose clause marker + object + verb.
segments.s623-g18: دل غمگنان is a noun phrase 'the hearts of the sorrowful' (دل = heart + غمگنان = the sad ones). شاد و بی‌غم are two coordinated adjectives ('happy and carefree') modifying دل; کنید (same 2pl ending as above) makes the whole clause an imperative 'make [them]...'. The coordinating و links the two adjectives.
segments.s623-g19: که begins an explanatory clause here ('for' / 'because' / 'that'). گیتی فراوان is a nominal phrase 'the world (in abundance)' and نماند is the present/simple poetic negative 'does not remain / will not last.' به کس is a dative phrase 'for anyone' (به marking the beneficiary). The clause states a general truth: the world's abundance will not remain for any person.
segments.s623-g20: بی‌آزاری functions as an adjectival/nominative command 'be harmless' (the copula 'باشید' is omitted as typical in poetry). و links items in a list. داد here is the noun 'justice' (not a past tense of دادن). جویید is a 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive 'seek.' the final و بس ('and enough') is a concluding particle meaning 'and that is sufficient.' The sequence gives two moral imperatives followed by a closing remark.
segments.s623-g21: بدین here functions as a fused prepositional phrase 'in/by this' (poetic 'بدین گیتی' = 'in this world'). اندر = 'in/within' and places the following noun. نشانه is the predicate noun 'a sign/mark' and منم ('I am') is the emphatic copular predicate. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: locative phrase + predicate + copula.
segments.s623-g22: سر راستی را is a definite object phrase: سر ('head/chief/essence') + راستی ('truth/uprightness') with the object marker را, specifying what follows. بهانه is a noun 'pretext/excuse/occasion' used predicatively with منم ('I am'), so the structure literally reads 'I am the pretext of the head/essence of truth'—i.e. 'I am the occasion/exemplar of uprightness.' The object precedes the predicate because of the object marker را and poetic word order.
segments.s623-g23: که introduces the clause ('that/for'). چندان is an adverbial/quantifier 'so much / so great.' سپه is 'army/host' and کرد is the simple past 'did/made' (3rd person). آهنگ من ('my intention/plan') appears after the verb by poetic inversion and functions as the agent/subject: literally 'my intention made so great a host.' The past-tense کرد ties the event to a concrete past action.
segments.s623-g24: هم is 'also/too' or 'together'; آهنگ here again means 'aim/intention.' این نامدار انجمن is a noun phrase 'this renowned assembly/company' (این = this, نامدار = renowned, انجمن = assembly). The whole segment reads 'my aim is also in accord with this renowned assembly'—'هم' stressing agreement or addition. Word order places modifier نامدار after the demonstrative and before the head انجمن, as is usual.
segments.s623-g41: هرانکس introduces a generic relative 'whoever'; که begins a relative clause modifying it. درویش باشد is a predicative phrase 'is a poor/dervish person' with باشد functioning as the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' in classical style. به شهر places the location 'in/at the city' after the predicate, the older word order typical of epic Persian.
segments.s623-g42: که continues the relative clause 'for whom / who', از روز شادی is a genitive-like phrase 'of a day of joy' ('از' = 'of/from' + روز شادی). نیابند is a negative third-person plural present/subjunctive form of یافتن ('to find/attain') meaning 'they do not find'. بهر here is an adverb/prepositional particle 'for / with respect to' linking purpose or circumstance.
segments.s623-g43: فرستید is an imperative/jussive 'send/dispatch' addressed to others; نزدیک ما is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'near us / to us' (placed after the verb in poetic order). نامشان is the direct object 'their name / their fame' (شان = their). The verb governs the object and locative complement in this order.
segments.s623-g44: برآریم is first-person plural subjunctive/optative 'let us raise / we shall raise' (a hortative construction). زان 'from that' refers back to the preceding clause; آرزو is 'wish/desire'. کامشان is 'their fulfillment/satisfaction' (کام here = fulfillment), so the phrase means 'let us raise [their name] and thereby fulfill their desire.'
segments.s623-g45: دگر means 'other/another' introducing a new class; هرک هستند پهلونژاد is an older word order for 'whoever are of hero-born stock' (هرک = 'whoever', هستند = 'are', پهلونژاد = 'of heroic lineage'). The predicate follows the relative phrase in this poetic inversion.
segments.s623-g46: که begins a relative clause 'who'. گیرند is present-subjunctive/plural of گرفتن 'to take/seize' used here idiomatically with یاد: 'گیرند ... یاد' = 'they take to memory / they remember' or 'are seized by the memory of'. از رفتن indicates the source 'from/of going/departure' (رفتن here = 'going, departure'); رنج یاد literally 'the memory of suffering' — together: 'who are seized by the memory of the hardship of leaving.'
segments.s623-g47: هم is an adverb 'also' or 'too'. از گنج ما is a prepositional phrase 'out of our treasure/treasury'. بی‌نیازی is a noun meaning 'freedom from need / sufficiency' and functions as the direct object, while دهید is the imperative/optative 'grant/give' addressed to the ruler or speakers.
segments.s623-g48: خردمند را places the definite object 'the wise man' before the verb using the object marker را. سرفرازی is the object complement 'honor/glory' and دهید is again the imperative 'give' — 'give honor to the wise.' Word order (object — complement — verb) is normal in Persian with را-marked objects.
segments.s623-g25: از ایدر برفتم به اندک سپاه is a compact past‑tense clause: از ایدر = 'from here' (prepositional phrase). برفتم is a poetic past of رفتن meaning 'I departed/left' (first person past). به اندک سپاه is a prepositional phrase of accompaniment/extent 'with a small troop' placed after the verb.
segments.s623-g26: شدند آنک بدخواه بد نیک‌خواه: شدند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they became' (or 'they were made'), and آنک is a relative/ demonstrative 'those who / that which' introducing the people concerned. بدخواه بد is a nominal phrase where بد (bad) intensifies the negative state of بدخواه (ill‑wisher). نیک‌خواه is the contrasting noun 'well‑wisher' — the clause says that those with ill will became bad (worse) while the well‑wishers were good (implied).
segments.s623-g27: یکی نامداری چو خاقان چین: یکی here means 'a certain' or 'one (certain)'; نامداری = 'a person of name/notability' (a famous man). چو is the simile 'like/as', and خاقان چین is the title + place 'the Khaghan of China' (title followed by place). The whole phrase introduces a famed person compared to that ruler.
segments.s623-g28: جهاندار با تاج و تخت و نگین: جهاندار = 'sovereign' or 'world‑ruler' (an epithet/appositive to the preceding). با = 'with' marking accompaniment or what he bears; the following nouns are listed by و 'and' (تاج = crown, تخت = throne, نگین = gem/signet). The phrase describes his regalia.
segments.s623-g29: به دست من‌اندر گرفتار شد is a passive/result phrase: به دست = 'into the hand(s) of' (agent/means), من‌اندر = 'in my power / by me' (emphatic possessive), گرفتار شد = 'was seized/captured' (گرفتار = seized/trapped + شد making it a past passive/result). The whole clause means 'he was taken/was captured by my hand.'
segments.s623-g30: سر بخت ترکان نگونسار شد: سر بخت is a compound 'head/foremost part of fortune' meaning 'the fortune/fate'; ترکان is the genitive 'of the Turks'; نگونسار = 'mournful/ill‑fated'; شد = 'became'. Together: 'the Turks' fortune became woeful/ill‑fated.'
segments.s623-g31: مرا کرد پیروز یزدان پاک: مرا is the object 'me' (with the object marker split here as م + را in the word list), کرد = 'made' (caused), پیروز = 'victorious' (object complement). یزدان پاک 'the pure God' is the agent/subject placed after the verb by poetic inversion, so the clause reads 'the pure God made me victorious.'
segments.s623-g32: سر دشمنان رفت در زیر خاک: سر دشمنان = 'the heads of the enemies' (possessive construction), رفت = 'went' (here used of being sent/laid away), در زیر خاک = 'into/under the earth' (locative). Together: 'the enemies' heads went beneath the ground.'
segments.s623-g97: A simple enumerative subject (زن و مرد و کودک) followed by a directional phrase (به هامون) and the past plural verb شدند. In classical Persian the past plural here describes a completed movement: 'they went/assembled at the plain.' The preposition به before هامون marks motion toward/into the plain.
segments.s623-g98: This clause states from where people emerged: از خانه بیرون شدند = 'they went out from the house.' The initial به هر کشور is distributive ('in/through every region') and associates the action with every land; word order places the distribution first for emphasis. بیرون (outside) follows the source phrase and شهدند is the same past-plural verb as above.
segments.s623-g99: همی + past verb خواندند forms a habitual/continuous past (epic imperfective) meaning 'they would sing/kept singing.' آفرین is an acclamation/noun 'praise' and نهان is an adverb placed after it meaning 'secretly' or 'inwardly.'
segments.s623-g100: بران is a contracted poetic prepositional phrase (بر آن = 'for him' / 'upon that') linking the praise to its addressee. دادگر شهریار جهان is a noun phrase: دادگر ('just' or 'just ruler') modifies شهریار ('king/suzerain'), and جهان ('of the world') is a genitival apposition — together: 'that just king/suzerain of the world.'
segments.s623-g101: ازان پس is a temporal connective 'after that' (از آنِ پس). به خوردن is a purpose/benefactive phrase 'for eating' and biārāstand (بیاراستند) is a past plural verb meaning 'they prepared/arranged' — together: 'they prepared for eating.'
segments.s623-g102: This is a simple coordinated list of what they requested: می‌و (wine/fruit), رود (here a musical instrument — a stringed/reed instrument), joined by و, followed by رامشگران ('comforters' i.e. soothing/entertaining people, likely musicians), and the past verb خواستند 'they desired/asked for.' The verb applies to the whole preceding list.
segments.s623-g103: یکی نیمه از روز is a quantitative phrase 'one half of the day.' خوردن is the noun/infinitive 'eating' functioning as the activity, and بدی here is an archaic/poetic past-tense or resultative element meaning 'they gave/they spent' (i.e. 'they devoted [that time] to eating'). The clause thus means 'they devoted one half of the day to eating.'
segments.s623-g104: دگر نیمه marks 'the other half.' ز is the poetic form of از ('from/of'), so 'دگر نیمه زِ کارکردن' = 'the other half from (was for) working.' کارکردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'working,' and the final بدی repeats the same archaic/resultative element 'they spent/devoted.' The pair of clauses contrasts how the day was divided between eating and work.
segments.s623-g81: This is an elliptical benediction: ز = 'from' (poetic) and دارنده = 'the possessor' (a title, here God or the Owner). بر جان آنکس means 'upon the life of that person' (بر + جان + demonstrative/relative آنکس), and درود is the noun 'blessing/greeting'. The verb of wishing (e.g. باد) is omitted, which is common in classical Persian poetry, so read as 'May there be blessings from the Possessor upon that person's life.'
segments.s623-g82: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. از مردمی = 'of/from the people' describes the material/origin; مردمی is an adjectival phrase 'of the people'. باشدش is the classical fused form of باشد + ـش (enclitic referring back to آنکس) and functions as the verb 'is/are his' or 'belong to him' with poetic word order (verb before its nominal predicate). تار و پود ('warp and weft') is the concrete subject/predicate placed after the verb by inversion; metaphorically it means the very fabric of his being.
segments.s623-g83: چو اندر نوشتند is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', اندر = 'on/in' (here 'on' or 'upon', marking the surface), and نوشتند is past tense 'they wrote' (3rd person plural). چینی حریر forms the object/locative phrase 'Chinese silk' describing what they wrote upon.
segments.s623-g84: This clause has an inverted poetic order: سر خامه را (the tip/head of the quill) with را as the definite object marker comes before the verb کرد ('made/did' in past 3rd sing.). مشکین دبیر is the agent 'the dark‑inked/black (or musk‑scented) scribe' placed after the verb; مشکین modifies دبیر. Read as 'the scribe made the nib of the pen black' (i.e. stained it with ink).
segments.s623-g85: به عنوان = 'under the title/heading' (idiomatic), followed by برش ('inscription/heading' or 'section') and then شاه گیتی 'King of the World' as the title. نوشت is the past verb 'he wrote'. The phrase is an account of what was inscribed: the title is given and then the epithets follow.
segments.s623-g86: This is a string of epithets. دل داد literally 'gave the heart' (دل = 'heart', داد = past of 'give') functioning here as an adjectival/epithetic phrase meaning 'giver of hearts' or 'generous‑hearted.' و links it to داننده 'knower', which is then modified by خوب و زشت 'good and ugly/bad' = 'knower of good and evil.' The coordination lists the king's qualities as written under the title.
segments.s623-g87: This is a compact invocation/wish: خداوند is 'God' (or 'the Lord'); بخشایش، فر و زور are a coordinated list 'forgiveness, royal glory (farr), and strength/might'. The verb of granting (e.g. دهد/بخشد or باد for optative) is omitted, a common feature in poetic prayers and benedictions.
segments.s623-g88: شهنشاه بخشنده is an appositive/title: 'the Shahanshah (King of Kings), the generous one.' بهرام گور is the proper name 'Bahram Gur' (گور here is the epithet 'Gur' — 'onager', part of his name). The whole segment names the beneficiary of the preceding prayer.
segments.s623-g113: This clause uses classical word order and a plural past verb: 'جهانی' (the whole/world) is the object/topic, 'به رامش' is a prepositional phrase 'toward tranquility', and 'نهادند' is the past plural of نهـا 'to place/set' meaning 'they set/turned'. 'روی' (literally 'face') here follows to indicate orientation — 'they set/turned (their) face toward calm'.
segments.s623-g114: This is a nominal verbless/resultative clause with the auxiliary/past verb 'شد' = 'became'. 'پرآواز' modifies 'میخواره' = 'loud‑voiced/dramatic drunkard', then 'شد' marks the change of state and 'شهر و کوی' ('city and quarter/alleys') is the domain that becomes so — 'the city and lanes became full of / were filled by the loud‑voiced drunkard.'
segments.s623-g115: 'چنان ... که' is the correlative pair meaning 'so ... that'. Here 'چنان' ('so') is followed by 'بد' functioning as the evaluative adjective (an intensifying/old poetic use of بد), then the clause continues with 'که' introducing the result: 'از بید و گل افسری' — 'a diadem (افسری) from the willow and the flower' (these natural things are personified as desiring a crown).
segments.s623-g116: This continues the result clause: the preposition 'ز' (= از, 'from, because of') introduces cause, 'دیدار او' = 'the sight/meeting of him', and 'خواستندی' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'desired/wished for' (functioning like a past tense verb here). The clause ends with 'کری' used as a predicative/object meaning 'insensibility/deafness' — poetically 'they were made senseless/overwhelmed by seeing him.'
segments.s623-g117: 'یکی' is the numeral 'one/a single', followed by the noun phrase 'شاخ نرگس' ('a stalk/branch of narcissus'). 'به تای درم' is a price phrase: 'به' = 'for/at', 'تای درم' is a small monetary amount (a dirham/pair‑dirham in older usage) — together 'for a dirham / for a small sum.'
segments.s623-g118: This is two short clauses compressed: 'خریدی کسی' = 'someone bought (it)' where 'خریدی' is a past form 'bought' and 'کسی' is an indefinite subject 'someone'. The following phrase 'زان نگشتی دژم' uses 'زان' = 'from that', 'نگشتی' (from گشت) = 'turned/wascome' and 'دژم' = 'sullen/grim' — 'from that, no one turned sullen' (i.e., the purchase did not make anyone gloomy).
segments.s623-g119: The clause begins with the causal preposition 'ز' = 'from/because of' + 'شادی' = 'joy'. The copular construction 'جوان شد دل مرد پیر' follows the poetic order: adjective 'جوان' + verb 'شد' + possessed noun phrase 'دل مرد پیر' — literally 'young became the heart of the old man' = 'the old man's heart became young.'
segments.s623-g120: This is a locative/result clause: 'به' = 'into/toward', 'چشمه درون آبها' = 'the spring, within the waters' (where the noun 'درون' specifies 'inside'), and 'گشت شیر' uses 'گشت' (became/turned) + 'شیر' — here 'شیر' is used metaphorically as 'lion' (strength) so the result is 'he turned into a lion in the spring waters' (i.e., regained vigor).
segments.s623-g49: کسی را که ... is a relative clause beginning with کسی را که “anyone who…”. را is the object/left‑dislocation/definite marker highlighting the person; که introduces the relative clause. فامست is an archaic adjectival/categorical predicate (فام + ‑ست, with ‑ست = ‘is’) describing that person; و links the next predication. دستش is دست + the enclitic ش ‘his/their’ and تهیست (تهی + ‑ست) is another adjectival predicate “is empty/devoid”, so the clause literally says “a person who is fām and whose hand is empty.”
segments.s623-g50: به هر کار is an adverbial phrase “in/for every deed” (به + هر + کار). The predicate that follows, بی‌ارج و بی فرهیست, uses the negative prefix بی‑ twice: بی‌ارج “without honor/worth” and بی فرهیست = بی + فرهیست “without nobility/distinction”; the final ‑ست is the copula ‘is’. Together they assert that the person described is unesteemed in every action.
segments.s623-g51: هم از گنج‌ماشان sets up an adverbial source/beneficiary: هم “also”, از “from”, گنج‌ماشان ‘their/our treasure’ (poetic compound). بتوزید is an archaic/distributed imperative or hortatory verb form (بـ‑ + توزید) meaning “distribute/dispense” (2pl imperative). فام here is the object — “a share/trace/reputation” to be given; the word order places the verb before its object in this poetic line.
segments.s623-g52: به دیوانهایشان is a dative/locative prepositional phrase “in/to their registers” (به + دیوانهایشان). نویسید is an imperative/plural present of نوشتن/نويسيد “write/record” and takes نام “name” as its object; the verb follows the dative phrase as is common in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s623-g53: ز یزدان بخواهید is a request clause: ز (poetic for از) “from”, یزدان “God”, and بخواهید is the 2pl imperative/subjunctive of خواستن “ask/entreat” (here functioning as “ask of God”). تا introduces a purpose/result clause “so that”; هم‌چنین “likewise/so” modifies what is requested.
segments.s623-g54: دل ما بدارد به آیین و دین: دل ما “our heart” (possessed noun phrase). بدارد is the present/subjunctive/capability form of داشتن used here with a hortatory/wish sense “may grant/let (it) have” or “may (our heart) hold/possess”; it connects to تا/بخواهید as the desired result. به آیین و دین is a prepositional phrase “to/for ritual and religion,” indicating attachment or orientation of the heart.
segments.s623-g55: بدین مهر ما is a prepositional/ablative phrase meaning “by/with this kindness (of) ours” — here بدین functions like به + این (archaic/poetic formation). مهر ما “our favour/kindness” is the noun phrase. شادمانی کنید is the polite/imperative 2pl “be joyful/rejoice” (شادمانی + کنید), so the clause urges taking delight in that favour.
segments.s623-g56: بران مهتران مهربانی کنید: بران (به + آن/بر آن) “toward them/for those” points forward to the beneficiaries; مهتران is an archaic plural meaning “great ones/chief persons” (here ‘leaders/elders’). مهربانی کنید is the 2pl imperative “do/show kindness” (مهربانی + کنید), so the clause instructs showing kindness to those important people.
segments.s623-g57: همان بندگان را is the definite/ topical object ('those same servants') marked by را. مدارید is a 2nd-person-plural exhortative verb meaning 'treat' or 'do (so)' and takes خوار as an object‑complement; together مدارید خوار literally means 'do not make (them) despised / do not hold (them) cheap' (the imperative sense is hortative/admonitory). Word order places the object before the verb and the predicative adjective خوار after the verb.
segments.s623-g58: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'), هست(ند) is the present plural copula 'they are', هم is the adverb 'also / too' emphasizing inclusion, and بنده کردگار is the predicate 'servants of the Creator' (کردگار is an older word for God/the Maker). The subject is carried over from the previous clause (those same people).
segments.s623-g59: کسی here means 'someone' (an indefinite agent). کش acts as an agentive element (worth reading as 'hauler' or 'one who pulls/carries'), so کسی کش = 'someone to haul/carry'. بود is the copula used in an optative/instructive sense ('let there be' / 'have'), and پایه سنگیان is the noun phrase 'the foundation of stones' (پایه = foundation/base, سنگیان = stones). The whole clause yields the instruction 'have someone carry the foundation stones.'
segments.s623-g60: دهد is a jussive/imperative‑flavored 3rd‑person singular of 'دادن' here meaning 'let (someone) give' or 'give'. کودکان را is the definite object 'the children' and به فرهنگیان is the prepositional phrase 'to the learned / men of learning' (فرهنگیان = cultured/learned people, teachers). The construction is a simple ditransitive pattern 'give X را to Y'.
segments.s623-g61: به دانش is an instrumental phrase 'by/through knowledge' (به + noun indicating means). روان را is the definite object 'the soul/spirit'. توانگر کنید is an imperative (2nd‑person plural) 'make (it) rich' where توانگر is a predicate adjective 'wealthy/rich' and کنید is 'make/do'. The order expresses 'make the soul wealthy by means of knowledge.'
segments.s623-g62: خرد را is the object 'wisdom' marked by را. ز تن is a source phrase 'from (the) body' (ز = from). بر سر is a locative 'upon the head', افسر is 'helmet' used metaphorically, and کنید is again the imperative 'place/make'. The clause literally says 'place wisdom, from the body, on the head as a helmet' — i.e. prefer wisdom as the head's ornament/defense rather than mere bodily adornment.
segments.s623-g63: ز چیز کسان is a genitive/partitive-like phrase 'from the things of others' (ز = from, چیز = thing(s), کسان = people/others). دور دارید renders as a present 2nd‑person plural verbal phrase 'keep (something) distant' — here دارید governs دور as its complement. دست (hand) is the theme: the clause means 'keep your hand away from other people's things' (word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s623-g64: بی‌آزار is an adjectival predicate 'harmless / inoffensive'. باشید is the 2nd‑person‑plural imperative/subjunctive 'be'. The conjunction و links the next predicate, یزدان‌پرست, a compound meaning 'God‑worshipper / pious one'. Together the clause gives two moral exhortations: 'Be harmless and be pious.'
segments.s623-g73: بزرگش = بزرگ + suffix ش (his/its); the adjective + possessive suffix functions as a nominal object: “his greatness.” مخوانید here is a 2nd-person plural subjunctive/imperative form used in Ferdowsi to express prohibition (‘do not call’); the root خوانیدن with the prefixed م produces the prohibitive sense common in epic style. کان introduces the following relative clause (‘whose/that’), linking برتری (the noun ‘superiority’) to the clause that follows.
segments.s623-g74: سبک modifies برتری in the previous segment and means ‘light/frivolous’ here; بازگردد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of بازگشت (to return/turn back) meaning ‘returns’ or ‘will revert’; سوی کهتری is a prepositional phrase ‘toward inferiority’ (سوی = toward, کهتری = inferiority). Together it expresses a conditional/result: if his superiority becomes light, it returns toward inferiority.
segments.s623-g75: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from.’ درویش here means ‘the poor/dervish’ (a person in need). چیزی functions as an indefinite object ‘anything/anything at all.’ مدارید is a 2nd‑person plural present/subjunctive used here in the sense ‘withhold/be stingy’ (in epic diction it carries the idea of not giving), and باز functions adversatively here (‘rather/on the other hand’), linking to the next clause.
segments.s623-g76: هرانکس که هست = ‘whoever/anyone who is’ (هرانکس + relative که + copula هست). از شما بی‌نیاز is a prepositional predicate ‘free of need with respect to you’ (بی‌نیاز = self‑sufficient). The whole clause names the people from whom one should withhold—those who do not need you.
segments.s623-g77: به پاکان = prepositional phrase ‘to/toward the pure (people).’ گرایید is 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive ‘turn (toward).’ و links the coordinated imperative نیکی کنید (‘do good’), where نیکی is a noun ‘good deed/goodness’ and کنید is the 2nd‑person plural imperative ‘do.’
segments.s623-g78: دل و پشت خواهندگان is the direct object (two coordinated nouns ‘the hearts and backs/supports of the petitioners/seekers’). مشکنید is a 2nd‑person plural prohibitive form (‘do not break’), the negative formed in epic Persian with the m‑ prefix; the verb follows the object here by poetic inversion.
segments.s623-g79: هران چیز = archaic quantifier ‘whenever/any thing’ (هران = هر آن). کان here links the clause (relative/archaic usage ‘that/which’). دور گشت is a past/resultative expression ‘became distant/went away’ (گشت = became/turned), and از پسند means ‘from approval/favour’; together: ‘any thing which becomes distant from favour.’
segments.s623-g80: بدان is the imperative ‘know (this)’ formed from ب- + دان (دانستن = to know). چیز repeats ‘that thing’ (referring back to the preceding clause). نزدیک باشد گزند is a verbless/poetic clause with inversion: گزند (‘harm’) is the subject and نزدیک باشد (‘is/shall be near’) the predicate; باشد is a subjunctive/optative form used here to state the fact ‘is near.’ The whole yields: ‘Know that harm is near that thing.’
segments.s623-g89: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward/to' and governs the following noun phrase. مرزبانان is the plural noun 'frontier-governors' and فرمانبران is a postpositive epithet modifying مرزبانان ('obedient' or 'submissive'); the sequence is a prepositional phrase 'toward the frontier‑governors who are obedient.'
segments.s623-g90: خردمند و دانا و جنگی are three adjectives joined by و ('and') that in this poetic order precede and modify the following noun سران ('leaders, chiefs'); the whole phrase reads 'wise and learned and warlike leaders.'
segments.s623-g91: به هر سو is a directional phrase 'to/every direction.' نوند و سوار و هیون is a coordinated list of moving units/creatures (an archaic/rare term نوند, then 'riders/horsemen' and 'beasts'), enumerated after the directional phrase.
segments.s623-g92: همی is the older Persian progressive/iterative particle (marks ongoing or habitual past) and precedes the past verb رفت ('went/set out'). با + نامه رهنمون is an instrumental/accompanying phrase 'with the letter as guide' (رهنمون functioning as 'guidance/guide' describing the letter).
segments.s623-g93: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when.' آن نامه آمد is a simple past clause 'that letter came/arrived.' به هر کشوری is the prepositional complement 'in/to each realm/country.'
segments.s623-g94: به هر نامداری و هر مهتری is a continuation of recipients: 'to every person of renown and every chief/elder.' The repeated هر ('every') gives distributive/emphatic force to each noun.
segments.s623-g95: همی گفت is an iterative/past construction 'kept saying' or 'would say.' هرکس is the indefinite subject 'everyone/whosoever,' and the following که introduces the quoted/exclamatory phrase یزدان سپاس ('God be praised' / 'praise to God').
segments.s623-g96: The particle که here introduces the content clause explaining what they praise: هست is the copula 'is,' and این جهاندار یزدان‌شناس is the noun phrase 'this sovereign/ruler, God‑knowing' (یزدان‌شناس a compound meaning a pious or God‑knowing person) serving as the predicate nominal.
segments.s623-g9: This clause is a compact past-tense statement: شناخَت (شناخت) functions as a perfect/past verb 'he knew/recognized'. همه نیکویها is the direct object 'all good things/all virtues' (word order: object before verb is common in classical Persian for emphasis). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from', and یزدان is the divine source 'God'. The implied subject ('he' or 'one') is omitted and supplied from the surrounding narrative.
segments.s623-g10: خرد is a noun 'wisdom' serving as the object of the verb جست (past of جستن here, 'he sought'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. با + noun marks accompaniment; با مرد دانا means 'with a/ the wise man' (مرد here = person/man). بساخت is a past-tense verbal form (ب + ساخت) meaning 'he made/formed' or 'he allied (himself)'; it is archaic/poetic but functions as a simple past verb connecting to the previous action.
segments.s623-g11: بدانید is an imperative (2nd-person plural or polite 2nd singular) meaning 'know' or 'be aware'. کز is the contracted relative/particle 'که + ز' meaning 'that/from which' here introducing a content clause. داد is the abstract noun 'justice' and جز is the restrictive particle 'except/only'. نیکویی is the noun 'goodness/virtue' completing the clause 'that from justice comes nothing but goodness'.
segments.s623-g12: نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive form ('should not come' or 'does not come' depending on context); it negates the following verb. نکوبد is an old/poetic subjunctive/negative form built on کوبیدن ('to strike') but here figuratively 'to beat/drive' or 'to force'; در introduces the locative 'into' or 'in', and بدخویی is the abstract 'ill-temper/unkindness'. Together the clause warns that goodness should not be driven into badness/ill-temper. Note classical Persian often uses these modal/negative present forms where modern Persian would use different constructions.
segments.s623-g13: هرانکس 'whoever' (indefinite relative) starts a relative clause; که is the relativizer 'who/that'. از here means 'from/among' (indicating subset). کارداران is the plural noun 'administrators/managers' (people entrusted with affairs), and ما is the possessive 'our'. The clause sets up 'whoever among our administrators...'; the subject is indefinite and will be qualified or continued by the next coordinated phrase.
segments.s623-g14: This segment lists coordinated descriptors: سرافراز is an adjective 'proud/honored' describing (implicitly) those officials; و is 'and'; جنگی is an adjective 'warlike/military' modifying سواران 'horsemen/riders' (سواران ما = 'our riders'). The phrase is elliptic (it omits a main verb here and will be completed by the following clause), a common poetic device to string parallel items.
segments.s623-g15: بنالد is a present/simple imperfect third-person form (ب + نالَد) meaning 'he laments' or 'he bewails'—classical Persian often uses ب- to form present/imperfect verbs. بیند is present/subjunctive third-person of دیدن 'he sees' (here narrative present). به جز is the phrase 'except' and introduces the two grim sights that follow (چاه 'well' and دار 'gallows' — دار here meaning 'gallows' in poetic diction). The clause describes a pitiable outlook: he laments and sees nothing except those fates.
segments.s623-g16: وگر is the conditional 'if' (poetic 'or if / but if'). کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain'. بر خاک افگنده is a passive/perfect phrase 'cast/thrown upon the ground' (افگنده = 'cast' or 'laid'). خوار is a predicative adjective 'despised/disgraced'. The structure gives the alternate fate: if he is slain, he is cast on the earth and is dishonored.
segments.s623-g33: جز از + noun phrase is an excluding expression: 'nothing except ...'. "مباد" is an archaic jussive/negative optative (lit. 'may it not be'), here giving a wish/prohibition about the speaker's condition. Word order places the excluded thing (بندگی پیشهِ من) before the jussive verb.
segments.s623-g34: Parallel structure to the previous clause: جز از + (راست اندیشهِ من) 'nothing but right thought'. "راست" qualifies "اندیشه" (straight/upright, i.e. honest) and "مباد" repeats the negative-optative wish. The line uses parallelism for emphasis rather than a new finite verb form.
segments.s623-g35: نخواهم is the first-person negative present/future of خواستن ('I will not / I do not wish'), here meaning 'I will not exact/accept'. خراج is the direct object ('tribute, land-tax'); از جهان specifies the source ('from the realm/world'). هفت سال is a temporal phrase ('for seven years') placed after the object.
segments.s623-g36: اگر ... بود‌گر is an emphatic conditional: اگر (if) + inverted auxiliary 'بود گر' (classical 'گر بود' = 'if there were'). زیردستی and همال are nouns naming subordinate people ('underling, retainer' and 'carrier/porter'). Together the clause means 'even if there were subordinates or porters'.
segments.s623-g37: به introduces the indirect object/recipient ('to'). هر is the distributive 'every'. کارداری here refers to officials/administrators; خودکامه‌ای is an indefinite/autonomous noun phrase 'any autocrat/self-willed ruler'. These are the addressees of the written note.
segments.s623-g38: نوشتند is simple past third-person plural 'they wrote'. بر پهلوی نامه‌ای is a locative prepositional phrase 'on the side/margin of a letter' (پهلوی = side; نامه‌ای = 'a letter', indefinite). The verb + prepositional phrase specify where the inscription was made.
segments.s623-g39: که introduces a reported-content clause (what was written). از زیردستان 'from the subordinates' marks the source/objects of what follows. جز از ... 'except from ...' introduces the permitted items: رسم و داد (here 'customary dues and justice/fair dues'). The noun phrase is the exception that may be taken from subordinates.
segments.s623-g40: نرانید and نگیرید are negative jussive/imperative-like forms embedded in the reported prescription introduced by که: they state what should not be done. نرانید (from راندن) is used here in the prohibitive sense connected to exacting/forcing; و links the two prohibitions. از بد نگیرید یاد literally 'do not take/receive the memory/notion of evil' — i.e. do not hold on to/accept blame or ill (the phrase warns against taking 'remembrance' or record of wickedness).
segments.s623-g1: سیوم روز is a temporal expression (an ordinal + 'روز') placed before the clause to mark when the action happened. بزم ردان is the direct object phrase ('a feast/banquet of ...'), and ساختند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they made/held' that closes the clause (time → object → verb word order). Note: سیوم is an archaic/poetic ordinal.
segments.s623-g2: نویسنده را is the object marked with the definite-object marker را. پیش here functions adverbially ('in front of / before them'), and بنشاختند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb meaning 'they seated / caused to sit' (an older/poetic form related to نشاندن). The object (نویسنده را) appears before the place/adverb and the verb.
segments.s623-g3: This clause begins with the durative/adverbial phrase به می‌خوردن ('while drinking'); اندر is a locative/adverbial modifier 'inside / among', چو is the conjunction 'when', and بگشاد is a past/perfective form 'opened / revealed'. چهر ('face') is the affected noun. The sequence sets a temporal/background situation: 'as they drank, when (his) face opened.'
segments.s623-g4: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone / a certain one'. نامه بنوشت is the verb phrase 'wrote a letter' with بنوشت as an archaic/poetic past form of نوشتن. شادان is an adverbial/adjunct meaning 'joyful / glad', and به مهر here links the action to 'affection / with a loving tone' (مهر is used adverbially). The clause describes the act of writing the letter and the mood/tone in which it was done.
segments.s623-g5: سر نامه is a locative/phrase 'at the head/top of the letter'. کرد آفرین is a verbal phrase where کرد is the past tense 'he made/did' and آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' — together 'he put/expressed praise'. از نخست is an adverbial phrase 'from the first / from the beginning', modifying the whole action.
segments.s623-g6: بران is a deictic/adverbial element ('thereon / upon that / for that one'), followed by the relative/cohesive particle کو (here introducing a relative clause 'who'). روان را is the object 'the spirit/soul' marked with را, به شادی adverbially qualifies the action 'with joy', and بشست is the past verb 'he washed' used metaphorically (i.e., 'he refreshed/filled (the spirit) with joy'). Word order places the relative introducer before the clause and the verb at the end.
segments.s623-g7: خرد is the grammatical subject 'wisdom'. بر دل خویش is a locative/possessive phrase 'upon his own heart' (خویش = 'his own' realized as خوی + ش), and پیرایه کرد is a compound predicate where پیرایه 'ornament/embellishment' is the object and کرد the past-tense verb 'he made/caused' — literally 'wisdom made an ornament on his heart' (i.e., wisdom adorned his heart).
segments.s623-g8: به رنج تن is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by the suffering/hardship of the body' (به used adverbially/instrumentally). از مردمی here indicates the beneficiary/relational domain 'for/of the people', and مایه کرد is the verbal phrase 'he made (himself) a m āye (source/resource)' — so the clause means he became a resource/benefit to the people through bodily toil.
segments.s623-g105: همی is a habitual/iterative marker meaning 'always/regularly' in epic narration. نو here functions adverbially as 'anew' or 'freshly'. به هر بامدادی پگاه is a time phrase ('at every dawn/morning') with the preposition به + quantifier هر + temporal noun بامدادی and a synonymous reinforcing noun پگاه.
segments.s623-g106: خروشی is a noun 'a cry/commotion'. بدی here functions as an archaic narrative verb meaning 'caused/produced' (an older form used in poetry to indicate action). پیش درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the king' (پیش = 'in front of/ before', درگاه = 'royal court/palace presence', شاه = 'king').
segments.s623-g107: که introduces a content/relative clause 'that/who'. هرکس که is the correlative 'whoever'. دارد here functions as an auxiliary giving a perfect/experiential sense ('has'), followed by the past-stem forms خورید and دهید to express 'has eaten and has given' (archaic poetic aspect).
segments.s623-g108: سپاسی is the noun 'praise/thanks' and ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of/for'. خوردن is the verbal noun (gerund) 'the eating'. به خود is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to himself'. برنهید is an archaic prohibitive/optative or negative directive built from بر + نهید meaning 'do not place/attribute' — here 'do not attribute praise to yourself.'
segments.s623-g109: کسی is an indefinite 'someone/anyone'. کش here is an agentive/relational element attached to the noun (literary/patterned usage: 'one who ...'). نیازست is an impersonal idiom meaning 'it is necessary' or 'one is in need'; together with آید ('comes/goes') it conveys 'if/when someone is in need, he comes to the treasure' (آید as main verb of motion). به گنج marks the destination 'to the treasury/treasure-house.'
segments.s623-g110: ستاند is the verbal stem functioning as the main verb 'takes/withdraws' (an archaic poetic verb form here). ز گنج is the source prepositional phrase 'from the treasure'. درم سخته پنج is the object 'five hard dirhams' (number + coin + adjective 'sakhte'='hard/solid/firm', postmodified in the usual Persian order).
segments.s623-g111: سه من is a quantity 'three man' (man = a unit of weight/measure); the provided lemma من also has the pronoun sense but here is a measure (sense not listed). تافته is an adjective meaning 'set apart/clarified/refined' placed before the noun (poetic adjective), باده is 'wine', and سالخورده is a postpositive adjective 'aged' (literally 'years-old').
segments.s623-g112: به رنگ introduces a simile 'in the color of'. گل نار is a poetic color-image ('flower of fire' → reddish/blazing color). و با رنگ زرد adds a parallel description 'and with a yellow color' (با + رنگ = 'with the color'). Word order places the color phrases after the quantified wine.

sentences.s623-s61: The world-seeker remembered the sovereign, who had seen the whole world and was thus happy.
sentences.s623-s33: Strive, and do not break our pact; uproot the bad root and bonds.
sentences.s623-s34: Seek refuge in God and give orders; make the soul a hostage to His love.
sentences.s623-s35: Do not seek to harm the neighbors, nor those great and substantial men.
sentences.s623-s36: Whoever, though insignificant, became evil and dominant, and surpassed the measure of lowliness.
sentences.s623-s9: Do your best to lessen sufferings; make the hearts of the sorrowful glad and carefree.
sentences.s623-s10: For the world will not remain plentiful for anyone; be harmless, seek justice, and that is enough.
sentences.s623-s11: In this world I am a sign; I am the pretext (occasion/exemplar) for uprightness.
sentences.s623-s12: For my plan raised so great a host; my purpose too is in harmony with this renowned assembly.
sentences.s623-s21: Whoever is a poor man in the city, for whom they find no day of joy.
sentences.s623-s22: Send their names to us; let us raise (their) reputation and thereby fulfill their desire.
sentences.s623-s23: As for others — whoever are of heroic stock — who take to heart the hardship of departure.
sentences.s623-s24: Also out of our treasure give (them) sufficiency; grant the wise honor.
sentences.s623-s13: I left here with a small troop; those who were ill‑wishing turned worse, while the well‑wishers remained good.
sentences.s623-s14: A certain renowned man, like the Khaghan of China: a sovereign with crown, throne, and gem.
sentences.s623-s15: He fell into my hands (was captured by me); the Turks' fortune became woeful.
sentences.s623-s16: The pure God made me victorious; the heads of the enemies went under the earth.
sentences.s623-s49: Women, men, and children went out to the plain; from every land they came out of their houses.
sentences.s623-s50: They would chant 'Afarin' in secret for that just sovereign of the world.
sentences.s623-s51: After that they set out provisions for eating; they asked for wine, stringed instruments, and soothing musicians.
sentences.s623-s52: One half of the day they gave to eating; the other half they gave to working.
sentences.s623-s41: May blessings from the Possessor rest upon the life of that person whose warp and weft are of the people.
sentences.s623-s42: When they wrote on Chinese silk, the scribe made the tip of the quill dark (with ink).
sentences.s623-s43: Under the heading 'King of the World' he wrote: 'Giver of hearts and knower of good and evil.'
sentences.s623-s44: May God grant forgiveness, glory, and strength to the generous Shahanshah, Bahram Gur.
sentences.s623-s57: They turned the whole world toward calm; the lanes and streets became full of a loud‑voiced drunkard.
sentences.s623-s58: So (it was) that, from seeing him, the willow and the flower longed for a diadem and were made senseless.
sentences.s623-s59: Someone bought one stalk of narcissus for a dirham; from that no one turned sullen.
sentences.s623-s60: From joy the heart of the old man became young; into the spring's waters he turned into a lion.
sentences.s623-s25: Anyone who is of no standing and whose hand is empty is without worth and without distinction in every undertaking.
sentences.s623-s26: Also from our treasure distribute a share (fām) to them; write their name in their registers.
sentences.s623-s27: Ask God so that He may likewise grant our heart (attachment) to ritual and religion.
sentences.s623-s28: Rejoice in this favour of ours; show kindness to those great ones.
sentences.s623-s29: Do not make those same servants contemptible, for they too are servants of the Creator.
sentences.s623-s30: Have someone haul the foundation stones; give the children to the men of learning.
sentences.s623-s31: Make the spirit rich through knowledge; place wisdom, taken from the body, upon the head as a helmet.
sentences.s623-s32: Keep your hand away from other people's possessions; be harmless and worship (the) God.
sentences.s623-s37: Do not call him great whose superiority, if it becomes light, will revert toward inferiority.
sentences.s623-s38: Withhold nothing from a dervish; rather, withhold from anyone who is self-sufficient toward you.
sentences.s623-s39: Turn to the pure and do good; do not break the hearts and backs of those who ask.
sentences.s623-s40: Whenever a thing has gone away from favour, know that harm is near that thing.
sentences.s623-s45: To the frontier-governors — obedient, and to the wise, learned, and warlike leaders.
sentences.s623-s46: In every direction footmen, horsemen, and beasts set out, carrying the guiding letter.
sentences.s623-s47: When that letter arrived in every realm, to every notable and every chief.
sentences.s623-s48: Everyone kept saying, 'Praise God that this ruler is God‑knowing.'
sentences.s623-s5: He knew that all goodness comes from God; he sought wisdom and allied himself with a wise man.
sentences.s623-s6: Know that from justice nothing but goodness comes; it must not be turned into ill-temper.
sentences.s623-s7: Whoever among our administrators is proud, and our warlike horsemen (likewise)...
sentences.s623-s8: He laments and sees nothing but the well and the gallows; or if slain, (is) cast upon the earth, disgraced.
sentences.s623-s17: May I have no profession but servitude, and may I have no thought but right (upright) thought.
sentences.s623-s18: I will not exact tribute from the realm for seven years, even if there should be underlings or porters beneath (me).
sentences.s623-s19: They wrote, on the margin of a letter, to every administrator and every autocrat.
sentences.s623-s20: That from the subordinates you should take nothing except customary dues and justice, and should not take any remembrance of evil.
sentences.s623-s1: On the third day they held a feast and had the scribe sit before them.
sentences.s623-s2: While they were drinking, when he revealed his face, someone joyfully wrote a letter with affection.
sentences.s623-s3: He began the letter with praise from the first, for him whose spirit he washed with joy.
sentences.s623-s4: Wisdom adorned his heart; by the toil of the body he became a resource for the people.
sentences.s623-s53: Each dawn anew, he would raise a commotion before the king's court.
sentences.s623-s54: That whoever has eaten and has given should not claim any praise for the eating to himself.
sentences.s623-s55: If anyone is in need, let him go to the treasury and take five hard dirhams from the treasure.
sentences.s623-s56: Three man of clarified, aged wine, of the color of a flame‑flower and of yellow.
